audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Numbers / Bemidbar wilderness-itinerary and land-dispossession text tradition, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Exodus departure, forty-two-station itinerary, Aaron's death date and age, plains-of-Moab endpoint, image/high-place destruction, land allocation, and threat concerning remaining inhabitants. Strong anti-colonial, anti-genocide, and memory-ethics safeguards required.

verses.restored: These are journeys of children of Israel who went out from land of Egypt by their hosts, by hand of Moses and Aaron.
Moses wrote their departures according to their journeys at mouth/command of YHWH; these are their journeys according to their departures.
They set out from Rameses in first month, on fifteenth day of first month. On day after Passover children of Israel went out with uplifted hand before eyes of all Egyptians,
while Egyptians were burying those YHWH had struck among them, every firstborn; and upon their gods YHWH executed judgments.
Children of Israel set out from Rameses and camped at Succoth.
They set out from Succoth and camped at Etham, which is at edge of wilderness.
They set out from Etham and turned back to Pi-hahiroth, which faces Baal-zephon, and camped before Migdol.
They set out from before Hahiroth and passed through midst of sea toward wilderness; they went a journey of three days in wilderness of Etham and camped at Marah.
They set out from Marah and came to Elim; at Elim were twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees, and they camped there.
They set out from Elim and camped by Sea of Reeds.
They set out from Sea of Reeds and camped in wilderness of Sin.
They set out from wilderness of Sin and camped at Dophkah.
They set out from Dophkah and camped at Alush.
They set out from Alush and camped at Rephidim, and there was no water for people to drink.
They set out from Rephidim and camped in wilderness of Sinai.
They set out from wilderness of Sinai and camped at Kibroth-hattaavah/Graves of Craving.
They set out from Kibroth-hattaavah and camped at Hazeroth.
They set out from Hazeroth and camped at Rithmah.
They set out from Rithmah and camped at Rimmon-perez.
They set out from Rimmon-perez and camped at Libnah.
They set out from Libnah and camped at Rissah.
They set out from Rissah and camped at Kehelathah.
They set out from Kehelathah and camped at Mount Shapher.
They set out from Mount Shapher and camped at Haradah.
They set out from Haradah and camped at Makheloth.
They set out from Makheloth and camped at Tahath.
They set out from Tahath and camped at Terah.
They set out from Terah and camped at Mithkah.
They set out from Mithkah and camped at Hashmonah.
They set out from Hashmonah and camped at Moseroth.
They set out from Moseroth and camped at Bene-jaakan.
They set out from Bene-jaakan and camped at Hor-haggidgad.
They set out from Hor-haggidgad and camped at Jotbathah.
They set out from Jotbathah and camped at Abronah.
They set out from Abronah and camped at Ezion-geber.
They set out from Ezion-geber and camped in wilderness of Zin, that is Kadesh.
They set out from Kadesh and camped at Mount Hor, at edge of land of Edom.
Aaron the priest went up Mount Hor at mouth/command of YHWH and died there in fortieth year after children of Israel came out from land of Egypt, in fifth month, on first of month.
Aaron was one hundred twenty-three years old when he died at Mount Hor.
Canaanite king of Arad, who dwelt in Negev in land of Canaan, heard of coming of children of Israel.
They set out from Mount Hor and camped at Zalmonah.
They set out from Zalmonah and camped at Punon.
They set out from Punon and camped at Oboth.
They set out from Oboth and camped at Iye-abarim, at border of Moab.
They set out from Iyim and camped at Dibon-gad.
They set out from Dibon-gad and camped at Almon-diblathaim.
They set out from Almon-diblathaim and camped in mountains of Abarim, before Nebo.
They set out from mountains of Abarim and camped in plains of Moab by Jordan at Jericho.
They camped by Jordan from Beth-jeshimoth as far as Abel-shittim in plains of Moab.
YHWH spoke to Moses in plains of Moab by Jordan at Jericho, saying,
Speak to children of Israel and say to them: When you cross Jordan into land of Canaan,
you shall dispossess all inhabitants of land from before you, destroy all their figured stones, destroy all their molten images, and devastate all their high places.
You shall take possession of land and dwell in it, for to you I have given land to possess it.

You shall inherit land by lot according to clans; to many you shall increase inheritance and to few you shall diminish inheritance. Wherever lot comes out for him, it shall be his; according to tribes of your fathers you shall inherit.
But if you do not dispossess inhabitants of land from before you, then those you leave shall be barbs in your eyes and thorns in your sides, and they shall distress you in land where you dwell.
Then it shall be that what I planned to do to them, I will do to you.

translation_notes: The itinerary turns movement into communal memory: departure, water, hunger, revelation, grief, and transition are mapped through place names.
Some stations and sequences differ from or supplement narrative itineraries elsewhere in Exodus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy; source and geography audit remain necessary.
Aaron's death is dated precisely and his age recorded, distinguishing his personal ending from continued priestly office.

Verses 52-56 command dispossession and destruction of Indigenous religious sites within an ancient conquest framework. They must never authorize modern colonial settlement, cultural erasure, forced conversion, ethnic cleansing, or destruction of sacred places.
The imagery of inhabitants as barbs and thorns can dehumanize populations and requires explicit restraint.
Land allocation by population and lot repeats Numbers 26 while remaining embedded in a morally contested conquest narrative.
safety_note: Never use Numbers 33 to justify colonialism, genocide, forced displacement, iconoclasm, destruction of Indigenous religion, or treating neighboring peoples as contamination. The itinerary may serve memory and accountability; the conquest commands remain ancient violence under categorical non-reenactment.
